lede: Didactic & Moral · คำสอน accounts for 175 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phrae (14% of the corpus for this genre), ahead of Chiang Rai and Phayao. Nearly all (94%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (88%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (9%). Dated witnesses run 1742–1974 CE (81 of 175 carry a date).

authored.title: Didactic & Moral
authored.body_html:
What this is

This is the wisdom-and-conduct literature: how to live well, told as maxim, admonition and proverb. The catalogue files all 175 witnesses cleanly as Didactics, and the titles fall into two clear registers.

On the learned side sit the Pali nīti classics — above all the Lokanīti, which appears here several times over: the great anthology of moral maxims that travelled from Indian nīti through Burma into the north, the standard gnomic reader of the region. Around it cluster the ovāda literature (owāda, admonition — Owata tipani, Owatanusasini katha) and the subhāsit (subhāsita, "well-spoken" sayings — the proverb collections).

On the vernacular side sits something warmer and more domestic: the folk-didactic of elders instructing the young. Pu thao son lan — "grandfather teaches grandchild" — Manglai son luk ("Mangrai teaches his children"), kham son teaching-verses, and the sayings of Phaya In (Indra) as folk wisdom-figure. Alongside them run texts on householder conduct (gihi-cariyā, Kihicaritta) — worldly ethics for the layperson, not the monk.

For the wider collection this is the ethical layer: adjacent to the canon but not soteriological, adjacent to the wichaa but not efficacious. It is where the tradition records its values rather than its doctrine or its power — the voice of the teacher and the elder rather than the scribe of scripture.

The shape of the collection

The physical profile is ordinary for a learned genre — 88% palm-leaf, 94% Tham Lanna, overwhelmingly Pali-and-Lan Na — and it skews late, the 20th century (46 dated witnesses) edging out the 19th, consistent with a living folk-wisdom still being copied within living memory.

But its geography is extraordinary, and is the real finding here — see the first note.

Notes

  • The flattest geography in the collection. No library dominates this genre: Phrae, Chiang Rai, Phayao and Lampang each hold about a seventh, Chiang Rai stands unusually high, and Wat Sung Men — which supplies 40–43% of the canonical and grammar corpora — holds barely 9% here. That evenness is not an accident of survival: proverbs and elders' teachings were popular and domestic, not a monastery monopoly, so they survive scattered across every muang rather than concentrated in one great library. This is one of the few genres whose provenance counts are a genuinely even regional sample.
  • One label, two registers. The Pali nīti / subhāsit classics and the vernacular pu thao son lan folk-didactic are quite different objects — learned gnomic anthology vs. domestic teaching-verse — collapsed under one heading. Splitting them is the obvious next pass, and would separate a translated Indian tradition from a native Tai one.
  • Boundary blur with law and astrology. Several volumes bind didactics to kotmai boran (old customary law) or to Holasat (astrology) — the household-manual pattern again, where conduct, law and the calendar share a single book. Check the raw metadata and cross-reference customary law when a title names both.
  • Skews late. The dated witnesses run from the 18th to the 20th century, weighted toward the most recent — folk wisdom kept being copied within living memory, the mark of a genre that stayed in domestic use rather than freezing into a fixed canon.
